findings.label: Does the deck include a clearly identified Problem slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as PROBLEM
findings.suggestion: You've got the problem statement buried in Slide 2 as part of a longer "Startup Information" section, but investors need it to hit harder as its own dedicated slide early in your deck. Create a standalone Problem slide right after your cover that leads with the three most painful inefficiencies for your buyer persona—delayed lead responses, fragmented agent networks, and lack of data-driven insights for foreign investors—then show the financial impact (e.g., "Traditional brokers close only 15-20% of qualified leads due to manual bottlenecks"). This makes the urgency visceral before you introduce MIRA as the solution.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Solution slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as SOLUTION
findings.suggestion: You've got your problem statement and business model locked down, but you're missing a dedicated slide that walks investors through exactly how MIRA works from the customer's perspective—the step-by-step experience that makes your solution different. Create a visual "How It Works" slide showing the six-step journey (Discovery via WhatsApp/chat, AI Matching, Valuation & Advisory, Virtual Exploration with AR/VR, Conversion & Closing, After-Sales Support) with icons or screenshots for each stage, so investors can immediately see the end-to-end flow and where your AI actually creates value versus traditional brokers. This makes your solution tangible instead of just conceptual.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Team slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 8, Slide 9, Slide 10

findings.label: Does the deck include a Financials slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as FINANCIALS
findings.suggestion: You have AED 800K in revenue from six months of operations—that's real traction—but investors won't know how to value it without seeing your unit economics. Add a one-page financials slide showing monthly revenue breakdown (which streams: commissions, developer partnerships, SaaS?), your burn rate, and a simple CAC-to-LTV calculation for at least one customer segment, even if it's preliminary—this transforms your traction from impressive-sounding numbers into a credible financial story.

findings.label: Does the deck include a Traction slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 6

findings.label: Does the deck include a Market slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as MARKET
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide showing the UAE real estate market size, growth rate, and your specific addressable market within it—right now investors only see a vague "$280B global commission pool" reference buried in your investment rationale. For example, create a slide titled "Market Opportunity" with: total UAE real estate transaction volume (e.g., AED 500B+ annually), the percentage handled by brokers vs. direct sales, your estimated SAM (e.g., AED 50B in brokerage commissions), and your SOM based on realistic market capture in year 1-3. This grounds your ambition in real numbers and shows you've done the homework on why Dubai/UAE is your

findings.label: Does the deck include a Cap Table or Funding slide?
findings.evidence: Section found at Slide 4

findings.label: Does the deck include a Competition or Competitive Landscape slide?
findings.evidence: No slide classified as COMPETITION
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide mapping MIRA against 3-4 specific competitors in UAE/MENA—name them (Property Finder, Bayut, Huspy), show side-by-side comparison of key features like AI lead qualification, AR/VR capabilities, pricing model, and market share, then explicitly state what makes MIRA defensible (e.g., "only platform with DLD API + developer CRM integration" or "70% conversion vs. industry 40%"). This turns the vague "crowded landscape" concern into a concrete proof point that you've thought through why you win.

findings.label: Does the deck claim AI, ML, or proprietary technology? If so, does it provide any technical substance — architecture, methodology, or differentiation — beyond marketing language?
findings.evidence: LLM returned non-JSON after retry
findings.suggestion: Right now you're listing "AR/VR + NLP + CRM" as your tech stack, but that reads like a feature checklist, not differentiation—investors will assume you're stitching together off-the-shelf tools. Instead, add one concrete technical detail that shows you've built something defensible: for example, "Our proprietary NLP model trained on 50K+ UAE property transactions achieves 94% accuracy in price forecasting vs. 78% for market benchmarks" or "Custom AR engine renders 3D property layouts in <2 seconds vs. 8-12 seconds for competitors." Pick the one technical achievement that's hardest for a competitor to replicate and lead

findings.label: Does the deck explain any defensible moat — IP, patents, proprietary data, network effects, or switching costs?
findings.evidence: unclear if MIRA has strong defensible IP or first-mover advantage
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide that maps your specific moat to each competitor you'll face—for example, "Property Finder handles listings, but MIRA's moat is our proprietary emotional intelligence layer trained on 10,000+ high-net-worth buyer interactions that competitors can't replicate without years of data collection" or "Our exclusive integration with Dubai Land Department creates switching costs because agents can't easily migrate their transaction history." Right now you're claiming deep integrations and a proprietary tech stack, but investors can't see why a well-funded competitor like Bayut couldn't build the same thing in 18 months.

findings.label: Does the deck describe the product's current development stage — idea, prototype, beta, live, or scaling?
findings.evidence: What is the current development status of MIRA's AI/AR platform? Is the technology live, beta, or roadmap?
findings.suggestion: Add a single-line status statement at the top of your Technology slide that says something like "Platform Status: Live in UAE with 3 active developer partners, processing 50+ transactions/month" — right now investors can't tell if you're running a pilot, a beta, or a full production system, and that ambiguity makes them question whether your 70% conversion rate and revenue claims are real or theoretical. The metrics you're citing only matter if people know they're coming from actual live usage, not a prototype.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a clear and logical customer journey or product workflow?
findings.evidence: Can you provide demonstrations or live access to the full customer journey, showcasing all claimed functionalities?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide that walks through a real customer interaction step-by-step—for example, "Buyer Journey: Initial Query to Offer" showing exactly what a buyer sees when they ask MIRA about a property (the AI response, AR visualization, ROI forecast output, and handoff to agent), with actual screenshots or a QR code linking to a live demo environment. This turns your claimed 70% conversion rate and 90% satisfaction from abstract metrics into something investors can actually visualize and understand.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any third-party technology dependencies that could represent a risk — cloud providers, APIs, open-source components?
findings.evidence: Is the AI model proprietary or built using third-party LLMs? Is there any IP protection?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated section on your tech stack that explicitly states whether your AI model is proprietary, fine-tuned, or built on third-party LLMs like OpenAI/Claude, and what IP you own versus license. For example: "MIRA's NLP engine is built on [LLM provider], fine-tuned on 50K+ UAE property transactions with proprietary training data; we own all custom models and have filed patents on our emotional intelligence layer and price-forecasting algorithm." This directly answers the investor question already flagged in your deck and removes ambiguity around your defensibility against competitors who might use the same base models.

findings.label: Does the deck address data privacy, security, or compliance relevant to the product?
findings.evidence: How is data privacy and security ensured, especially for high net worth and institutional clients?
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated security & compliance section that spells out exactly how you protect high-net-worth client data—for example: "All client financial profiles and transaction data encrypted end-to-end with AES-256; SOC 2 Type II certification in progress; compliance with UAE DLD data residency requirements and GDPR for UK/EU clients." This directly answers the investor question and shows you've thought through the specific regulatory landscape you're operating in, not just acknowledged it as a risk.

findings.label: Do the financial projections include stated assumptions — growth rate basis, customer acquisition assumptions, or cost structure logic?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the deck state the current burn rate and runway in months?
findings.evidence: SKIPPED: Target sections confirmed missing by Pass 1

findings.label: Does the deck provide unit economics — CAC, LTV, LTV:CAC ratio, or payback period?
findings.evidence: Please provide CAC vs. LTV data per customer type (B2B and B2C).
findings.suggestion: Add a new slide showing your actual unit economics split by B2B (developer partners) and B2C (individual buyers): for each segment, show how much you spend to acquire one customer (CAC), what lifetime value they generate (LTV), and your payback period. For example, "B2B Developer CAC: AED 5K | LTV: AED 120K | Payback: 2 months" versus "B2C Buyer CAC: AED 800 | LTV: AED 8K | Payback: 6 months" — this tells investors immediately which segment is more efficient and sustainable.

findings.label: Does the deck provide evidence of paying customers — not just users, pilots, or sign-ups?
findings.evidence: What proportion of claimed AED800K revenue is recurring, and what is derived from one-off deals?
findings.suggestion: Break down your AED800K revenue into two clear numbers on the slide: how much is recurring monthly/annual contracts with real estate developers or agents, and how much came from one-off transactions or pilots—for example, "AED550K recurring (Emaar partnership: AED25K/month), AED250K one-time." This instantly shows investors you have predictable, repeatable revenue, not just a spike from a single deal.

findings.label: Does the deck describe a specific, credible go-to-market strategy — not just 'sales and marketing'?
findings.evidence: What is your go-to-market strategy in neCw geographies (e.g., expansion to Saudi Arabia, UK)? How do you plan to acquire customers in these markets – organic vs. paid strategies? How do you plan to compete with established digital brokerage platforms and portals? Is there an internal sales team oOr partner-led channel strategy?
findings.suggestion: Right now you're listing questions investors will ask, not answering them with your actual strategy. Replace this with a concrete plan: for Saudi Arabia specifically, state whether you're partnering with the top 3 developers (like Emaar or Damac equivalents), launching a paid Google/Facebook campaign targeting expat investors, or embedding MIRA into existing portals like Bayut — then show the projected CAC and timeline for each channel, so investors see you've already mapped out exactly how you'll win each market.

findings.label: Does the deck show month-over-month or year-over-year growth with specific numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a simple growth chart to your Traction slide showing your AED800K revenue claim with month-by-month progression over the last 6-12 months — for example, a line graph showing Jan: AED50K → Feb: AED75K → Mar: AED120K, etc., so investors can see the actual trajectory and calculate your MoM growth rate themselves. This transforms a single number into proof of momentum.

findings.label: Does the deck mention customer retention, churn rate, or NPS?
findings.evidence: Please provide customer feedback, case studies, or NPS scores from early adopters.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any named customer logos, case studies, or testimonials?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a new slide showing 2-3 named developer or brokerage partners you're already working with, their logos, and one concrete metric from each (e.g., "Emaar Properties: 150 property listings processed monthly" or "Azizi Developments: 8% conversion lift on agent-assisted inquiries"). This transforms your AED800K revenue claim from abstract to credible and gives investors confidence you've already solved the customer acquisition problem.

findings.label: Does the team have a gap in a critical function — for example no technical co-founder for a tech product, or no sales lead for a B2B company?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong functional coverage across finance, growth, and governance, but you're missing a dedicated technology leader on your core team—someone who owns the product and platform development for your ecosystem hub. Add a CTO or VP of Engineering to your leadership roster with specific experience building marketplace or community platforms, so investors see you have technical depth to execute the infrastructure you're describing across all these services.

findings.label: Does the deck provide relevant prior experience for each founder — domain expertise, prior startups, or industry background?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Right now your team slide shows expertise areas but no founder backstory — investors need to know why you're the right person to build this. Add a brief bio for yourself (the CEO) that includes one concrete win: for example, "Founded [previous startup] which reached [milestone] before exiting" or "Led [specific role] at [company] where I built the ecosystem partnerships now powering Deal360" — this grounds your credibility in real experience, not just job titles.

findings.label: Does the deck mention any advisors, board members, or notable backers with relevant expertise?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: You've got strong individual experts listed, but investors need to see who's actually leading your company—add a dedicated "Leadership Team" section at the top of slide 10 that names your CEO, co-founders, and key executives with their backgrounds (for example: "Ahmed Hassan, CEO — 15 years in UAE startup ecosystem, previously founded XYZ that exited to ABC"). Right now it looks like you're a service provider with mentors rather than a company with a clear leadership structure, which makes it hard for investors to know who's accountable for execution.

findings.label: Does the deck mention founder vesting schedules or equity arrangements?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a new section to your Team slide that shows the cap table or founder equity breakdown—something like "Founder Equity: [Founder Name] 40%, [Founder Name] 35%, [Founder Name] 25%" along with a note on vesting (e.g., "4-year vest with 1-year cliff"). Investors will ask this in every meeting anyway, so getting ahead of it signals you've thought through incentive alignment and prevents awkward pauses when they dig into governance.

findings.label: Does the deck operate in a regulated industry — fintech, healthtech, edtech, legaltech — without mentioning relevant licences, compliance, or regulatory approvals?
findings.evidence: MIRA positions itself as an AI-powered real estate super agent that addresses these pain points by providing instant, 24/7 service. Leveraging smart algorithms, CRM integration, and real-time valuation tools, MIRA manages the entire client journey—from lead qualification to post-sale support.
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide on regulatory compliance and licensing that covers three specific items: (1) confirmation of which UAE real estate brokerage licenses or certifications MIRA currently holds or is pursuing (e.g., RERA registration, DLD approval for AI agent operations), (2) how MIRA handles data privacy for international investor information under UAE and target-market regulations, and (3) any regulatory approvals already secured from Dubai Land Department or equivalent bodies in Saudi Arabia and UK. For example: "MIRA holds [X license] from RERA as of [date], is in process for [Y approval] by Q2 2025, and complies with GDPR/local data residency

findings.label: Does the deck mention any pending litigation, IP disputes, or legal encumbrances?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Add a dedicated slide addressing legal and regulatory compliance that covers three specific areas: (1) any pending or resolved disputes with DLD, developers, or regulatory bodies in UAE/Saudi Arabia/UK, (2) confirmation of required real estate and AI agent licenses you've obtained or are pursuing, and (3) data privacy certifications (like GDPR compliance for international investors). For example, you could state: "Regulatory Status: UAE Real Estate Agent License (pending Q4 2025) | DLD API Integration Agreement (signed Sept 2024) | No pending litigation or IP disputes | GDPR-compliant data handling for EU/UK investors." This directly addresses investor concerns about hidden legal risks that could derail

findings.label: Are there any numerical inconsistencies across slides — for example revenue figures that contradict each other, or growth rates that do not match the underlying numbers?
findings.evidence: NOT_FOUND
findings.suggestion: Good news—your numbers are internally consistent across the slides, so you don't have a mathematical contradiction problem. However, you're missing a critical bridge: the AED 800K revenue claim needs a clear breakdown showing which of your six revenue streams generated it. Right now investors see the number but can't map it to your business model, which makes it feel disconnected from your operations. Add a simple table showing something like "Commission Share: AED 400K | Developer Partnerships: AED 250K | Ancillary Services: AED 150K" so the revenue feels real and traceable to actual customer activity.

findings.label: Does the deck make any extraordinary claims — market leadership, unique technology, or exceptional growth — without supporting evidence?

findings.label: Is the deck professionally presented — consistent formatting, no spelling errors, no placeholder text?
findings.evidence: TFFhaae uudggateeatt c TToneetccahhinnnsoo, hllooerggeyyin AA, inns nninuuteaanll dRReeedpp sooolrrettly
findings.suggestion: You've got corrupted text scattered throughout your footer—that garbled string "TFFhaae uudggateeatt c TToneetccahhinnnsoo..." appears on nearly every slide and completely undermines your professionalism. Find and replace that entire corrupted line with a clean, single-sentence legal disclaimer like "This document is confidential and intended solely for the recipient" or simply remove it if it's not essential. This one fix will instantly make your deck look polished instead of broken.
